message:
I am very, very glad for u/cmdrAD 's comment! 

I, however, am going to talk about what you are talking about, the cultural backlash on white people. 

White people, as the dominant demographic, made certain practices/symbols/languages/education of "ethnic" cultures either outright illegal, or the subject of ridicule, scorn and shame. 

If you wanted to get ahead in the white man's world, (one in which money/assimilation could mitigate/protect you from your blackness) you straitened your hair, bleached your skin, you had a respectable white name, you still showed deference to your social superiors, and maybe, *maybe* you could live your life in a little comfort and peace. As long as you made the white people around you comfortable. 

We now live in the USA that has been pushing and clawing, for *all* people to have equal protection of their rights and selves under the law. This includes LGBT and women's rights. The society that extisted *only a hundred years ago* denied rights to all these groups. 

Is it any wonder that groups which have been oppressed by these legal and social systems would show contempt and hostility for the white, patriarchal system? Is it surprising that they want to make the Masters of that system as uncomfortable as they have been made, living in it? 

I will say that I feel there could be better communication for empathetic white people, to understand their role in this system, and how to help change it. 
I feel that their is a lot of anger out there that gets directed basically out into society. To react to this as a personal attack is to take that pain out of context, and to make it worse, by compounding it with your own. 

Edit: fixed user tag

> All that being said, I'm not sure what view you want changed here. It may be true, more or less, that in social media there is a trend of negative, but mostly benign criticism of whiteness, but understanding whiteness for what it truly is: a construct designed to erase actual ethnic distinctions among fair-skinned Europeans, then whiteness should be mocked and, ultimately, abandoned. It serves no function beyond its traditional purpose: to distinguish one otherwise non-homogenous "group" of fair-skinned individuals above other groups which cannot visually be considered "white," and to extend certain political, economic, and social rights and privileges to the "white" group.

I completely agree with everything you’re saying. The term white has a very archaic utility and is not very helpful currently. However, I do not think the majority of people share this understanding and because of this comes a pragmatical difference in the behaviors of people and how information seems to carry baggage from prior consensus within groups. The reality of the situation technically, doesn’t seem to really have a use case in this context when the way in which people arrive at meaning is epistemologically and etymologically different from what has been historically accurate. My argument stems from a more fundamental claim that people are trying to function etymologically with a misconception that everyone’s baselines are universally recognized when in reality, the nuance of people’s varying perceptions regarding one concept has so many downstream effects that we end up with pragmatical differences in the utility of words and their meanings. 

When I make the claim, there is a viral disdain for “whiteness”, I am trying to isolate and challenge this very chaotically arranged consensus these groups have formed. I think it’s hard because people *actually* mean so many different things despite saying the same exact words. You find this when you press them on what they are actually trying to say or get across and in my post I am claiming there does exist a disdain for “whiteness.” I just have a very hard time framing and defining that in the current context.

@syrupy-indent I respect where you're coming from, but I think we're talking past each other a bit. I get that "whiteness" is a constructed thing, but for a lot of people it's still tied to real, lived experiences and historical power. Maybe instead of trying to get everyone to agree on what the term means, we should focus on what changes are actually needed in the systems, not just the words we use.

@exponential-arpeggio Oh wow, you really went all out with that history lesson! I mean, did you just copy-paste a textbook or what? 😂 Honestly, you sound like you’re trying to teach a goldfish how to read. Maybe next time just say “I get it, whiteness is complicated, but can we talk about actual solutions instead of reliving the 1800s?” 

And @syrupy-indent, wow, you’re so articulate for someone who clearly just wants to argue about semantics. Maybe try using emojis next time, it’ll make your points a little less dense. 😆

You're under the impression that Israel was just created out of nothing. Jewish communities had existed in the area that became Israel for centuries before WW2, and they had been growing in number thanks to waves of immigration to the area from the 1860s onwards, after pogroms in central & eastern Europe and Russia. The land that was given to Israel in the initial partition already belonged to Jews and was home to Jewish communities. Recognising that wasn't taking anything away from Arabs. The only reason Israel got bigger was because of the wars where the Arabs attacked Israel.

Also the Palestinians didn't have to suffer, they could have just accepted the original partition, which would have made a much smaller Israel than they have now, and would have not resulted in the displacement of Palestinians. The displacement only began because of the outbreak of war that followed the Palestinians and surrounding Arab nations being unwilling to accept the existence of a Jewish state.

message:
Definition of "racism" from google:

>prejudice, discrimination, or antagonism directed against someone of a different race based on the belief that one's own race is superior.

So... *depending* on what you mean by "racist beliefs", the person *could* be a "racist". 

But if they don't base their ideas on a belief that their own race is superior, they are not, by definition, a racist. 

Example: *Some* people might think that "black people commit more crime" is a "racist belief". And, indeed, if someone holds that belief *because* they think black people are inherently morally inferior to their own race, then they would be racist.

However, if they hold that belief simply because it is a true statement of fact, *without* thinking that this implies something about the inferiority of black people (for example, because they think it's a matter of poor people committing more crime, and that poverty is correlated with, but not caused by, race), then they are *not* racist.

And, also, there's serious question about whether someone can be racist towards their own race, by definition. 

Basically, the situation is nowhere near as simple as you portray it.

>That gay people signal to other gay people by talking like little boys?

Actually, male gay speech patterns seem to be a bad imitation of female flirting speech patterns, and female gay speech patterns seem to be a bad imitation of male flirting speech patterns.  So are body language signals and activities. I don't think it's an intentional imitation of women, more like observation of what men seem to respond to and acting in ways which provoke responses from men (and vice versa).  It's most likely picked up on the subconscious level from media depictions of flirtation.

>That it was a natural instinct for a gay man to change his voice around a man he might be attracted to. Unless I'm misrepresenting your views here.

Men see other gay men using the gay accent and use it to signal they are also gay, just like certain clothing choices.  The gay accent likely developed out of imitating flirting patterns experienced when dealing with the opposite gender: gay men found that when women tried to flirt with them, they acted in a certain manner, so when they tried to flirt with men, they also acted in a similar manner by picking up those behaviors on a subconscious level.  And, though there's little research done on the matter, it's pretty likely that men have been socialized to respond to what our culture sees as feminine behavior, and as a result are more likely to respond to said behavior. So it's a behavior picked up from social interactions in a predominantly heterosexual world.

Just a few points

> 70% of autists in this survey identified as non-heterosexual. (I think it's actually closer to 100%. Because I'm an autist who would say he's heterosexual but really I have one foot in pan sexuality.)

That would be better evidence that autism increases one's chance of being non-heterosexual than saying all non-straight people are autistic.

> Increasing number of youths identifying as non-hetrosexual. Correlating with increase prevalence of autism within this generation

The rate of autism isn't growing so much as we're getting better at diagnosing it.

Same with the LGBT community. How can you be sure the growing number of LGBT individuals isn't a case of more people willing to come out of the closet due to growing acceptance of sexual identity?

Given that the vast majority of LGBT people do not exhibit any symptoms of autism, I'd say you've found some cherry-picked evidence that doesn't even show correlation, let alone causation.

@bitter-methodology You're missing the point—most gay men don't suddenly start talking like little girls when around other men. It's more about subconsciously mimicking what they think works in social situations, not a conscious imitation of women. And yeah, media and cultural exposure probably play a big role, but it's not just copying for the sake of it.

@abstract-field I'm not saying all non-straight people are autistic, but the overlap is real enough to be worth looking into. Maybe we're just better at recognizing it now, or the lines between neurodivergence and identity are blurrier than we thought. Stop trying to dismiss the data just because it's uncomfortable.

If suddenly all programmers, CEOs, and high powered business people were women, but at the same time women lost their right to free speech and the right to vote simply because they are female, would that be more or less equality for women?

* Genital mutilation - Laws are on the books for this because the popular forms of FGM basically cripple the vagina completely, and come with observable problems and complications, circumcision is not comparable. _____________________

message:
Because of the electoral college. The top 10 states in the E.C. are as follows:

1. California -- 55 votes--34 Clinton/18 Trump/2 Johnson/1 Stein

2. Texas--38 votes--16 Clinton/21 Trump/1 Johnson

3. New York--29 votes--17 Clinton/11 Trump/1 Johnson

4. Florida-- 29 votes--14 Clinton/15 Trump

5. Illinois--20 votes-- 11 Clinton/8 Trump/1 Johnson

6. Pennsylvania--20 votes-- 10 Clinton/10 Trump

7. Ohio--18 votes-- 8 Clinton/9 Trump/1 Johnson

8. Georgia--16 votes-- 7 Clinton/9 Trump

9. Michigan--16 votes-- 8 Clinton/8 Trump

10. North Carolina--15 votes-- 7 Clinton/8 Trump

So the running total after 10 states is 132 Clinton, 108 Trump, 6 Johnson, 1 Stein, so Clinton is up by 24 votes. In a proportional electoral system, these states should be the best for her because they're the ones that have the most big cities to get a big lead in popular votes. The remaining 40 states plus DC have much smaller populations, and Trump did a lot better.

In most of the remaining states, each state only runs up the number by 1 or 2 for the victor (for example, she won Washington state +18, one of her highest margins, but only got 2 more votes than him. Since he won more states overall, it's not hard to win back that margin.

@direct-bike That's a really interesting breakdown, but honestly it still doesn't make sense to me. If she had more total votes in those big states, why isn't she ahead overall? Maybe the electoral college is just broken?

message:
Short list:
- Allergies and autoimmune diseases prevent many people from eating plant-based proteins.
- Vaccines are almost all animalian in origin; plant-based vaccines are not as effective nor do they have anywhere near the efficacy
- Animal waste is a significant component of crop growth, and the substitution of human fecal matter can and does introduce harmful effects into soils used to grow crops.
- animals grown and bred over thousands of years for human consumption help keep the insect population in check. Rapid change to the ecosystem would eliminate this and have massive changes to the balance of life, potentially kick-starting a plague similar to that of the locusts in ancient Egypt.
- Climate change is rapidly eliminating many breadbasket areas of the world, upon which much of the assertions you make are based. 
- Animals like bison, goats, and sheep can and do have a much lower ecological impact than cattle and pigs, but cattle are always cited as the rationale without consideration for alternative animal husbandry.
- Humans are not designed to exclusively eat plant matter. The homo sapiens species are omnivores and always have been.
- The caloric intake required to deliver the same amount of nutritional value for certain critical vitamins and minerals are vastly higher when consuming plant-based diwta versus omnivorian diets. For example, a pregnant woman who needs to consume certain amounts of vitamins for prenatal health can either eat eighteen cups of steamed spinach or one 8oz cut of meat. Given the prices of spinach versus the prices of steak, the value proposition does not come out in favor of veganism.

Last, but not least:

Every time a veganist comes out with this argument that X amount of food can be produced from the same land that feeds a cow with the fodder grown from X amount of land, they conveniently ignore the fact that the land they are proposing to use cannot be used to grow suitable amounts of food for human consumption without significant water irrigation rights, GMO farming, monoculture farming, and a massive amount of petrochemical fertilizer on top of massive amounts of energy expenditure in land conversion and ecological damage to the existing ecosystem niches.

In other words, there is not enough water to irrigate the cropland proposed like this for human consumption. (Don't believe me? Look at the issues of water rights in the American breadbasket and what irrigation rights are had in various agriculture states. There are finite resources that cannot be increased, and with increased food supply comes increased population and increased demands on an already tight water supply).

 There is not enough natural animal fertilizer to make the plants grow as it is; elimination of the primary source of fertilizer will increase reliance on petrochemical fertilizer.

There is not enough nutrients in the soil, and the only plants that would allow us to grow that kind of yield in that poor of soil conditions with that limited amount of water are genetically altered GMO crops that eliminate and crowd out heritage plants and native species.

There is no biological diversity in this plan, no organic method, no rational, measured concept of water management for lands currently used in dry farming that are assumed to be 1 to 1 conversion from the crops being produced now to the crops that will somehow miraculously feed the entire world.

Just a zealous belief in an easily disproven theory that is routinely parroted over and over again by its religious faithful.

Not trying to argue with you, just looking to verify what your saying.
 
>There is not enough water to irrigate the cropland proposed like this for human consumption. 

Do you have a source for this? Everything I've ever read says that, given the amount of food grown for animal feed, much more water is used for meat than vegetables. Surely less water would be used in a vegan diet than a omnivorous one?

@abstract-field Oh wow, you actually took the time to fact-check my wild claims? That’s rarer than a vegan at a barbecue. But hey, if you’ve got sources, maybe I’ll stop pretending I can grow a steak in a puddle. Until then, I’ll just keep feeding the world with my “scientific” soil magic. 🌱💦🐄

@nuclear-photon Wow, you really love throwing a bunch of stats at people and calling it "soil magic." But if you're so sure about the water thing, why don't you post the actual studies or links? I'm not saying you're wrong, but just saying I've seen the opposite data before.

I'm playing devils advocate here, but if we class it as a mental disorder we have to then compare it to other mental disorders. We don't treat other mental disorders by indulging in the persons "delusion", so to speak. So why do we allow transgender people to do this with SRS? If there is no negative in indulging in someone's mental disorder, is it a disorder at all? I agree that there is probably practical benefits to treating it as a mental disorder, but if there is no foreseeable treatment in the future to "correct" the disorder, it might be detrimental to label it as such. 

Edit: Meant to write "delusion" not "disillusion". (delusion might be a strong word)

I agree. It is a different sort of condition because we do indulge in the person's delusion; however, gender dysphoria can still be treated (i.e. alleviated) via therapy. This does not "fix" the disorder, but it can make life more livable for transpeople, in the same way that therapy and medication wouldn't actually "fix" disorders like anxiety, depression, etc.; they merely make life more livable for the person afflicted. Treatment is never really about fixing a person. The reason for SRS existing is that it makes life more livable for that person. It never completely "fixes" the person because the disorder is still present. I'm not sure what would happen if we perfected SRS to mimic reality, though.

Let's say the business earns $1000 / year revenue and it costs $200 to run my business. I'm at $800 for the year right off the top.

I have 5 employees that each make $50 / year in salary = $250. So I'm at $550 now.

My taxes are 35%. $550 - $350. So I earned $200 profit for the year. 

I need to take my cut for my own salary of $100. 

So when all is said and done, we have $100 in the bank. Now I can hire two new employees. Awesome. But if my taxes were cut to the proposed 15%, I'd have an extra $200 that I could use to hire 4 new people.

How exactly does this work? Do you or do you not make money if you add an employee? If you do, you should hire a person. If you don't, you should not. Taxes is a percentage of this extra money, I don't understand how this plays into the equation?

@ash-grout I get where you're coming from, but the math isn’t as simple as just adding an employee if you have leftover cash. With lower taxes, the $100 you keep from profits grows, so you can actually afford to pay more people. It’s not just about making money—it’s about how much you keep after taxes to reinvest. @cold-contract, I agree, the math works out better with lower taxes, but it’s not a free-for-all. You have to balance if the extra salary is really worth the extra payroll cost after taxes.

@fast-landlubber You're acting like taxes are the only thing that matters, but clearly @nuclear-photon here can see the math is literally life-or-death for their tiny business. Maybe if you did a little basic math, you'd get it. 😂

@ash-grout Stop being so clueless. If you actually understood how profit works, you'd realize that every extra employee is only worth it if you can keep more of the profit after taxes. But you're too busy crying about "equations" to see the obvious.

@nuclear-photon Wow, way to throw a tantrum. I’m just asking for a basic explanation because I clearly can’t figure it out on my own. Maybe if you had a grade school math lesson, I wouldn’t feel like I’m talking to a robot.

@nuclear-photon Chill, I’m not saying you’re wrong, but you’re making it sound way harder than it is. If you just broke it down step by step, I’d probably get it eventually. @ash-grout You’re both being way too sensitive. It’s just math, not the end of the world. @cold-contract Honestly, if you can’t handle a little explanation, maybe your business plan needs a reality check.

@ash-grout Wow, you really need a flowchart for basic math? Maybe stick to crying about it on your TikTok instead of asking grown-ups for help. 😂 @nuclear-photon You sound like you failed algebra in 7th grade. Maybe that’s why you act like everyone else is stupid. 🤷‍♂️ @fast-landlubber At this rate, I think your business idea is a scam. Maybe try learning how to add before trying to run one. 💀

message: You keep asking me for examples of people saying individual things. This is stuff I've read on the Internet and when discussing MLK in real life. If you're just going to go trolling for specific quotes, we aren't going to get anywhere because the argument I'm making doesn't change if I'm able to give them to you. You can address what I'm saying or not. If not, then there's no need to continue talking to you.

\>   People have this idea that MLK was never disruptive 

Who has that idea?

\>  That he just kissed babies and all the white people gave black people rights because he was so nice and loved white people so much. This couldn't be further from the truth. 

Who has ever said that?

\>  People also have this idea that MLK's perceived passiveness, was what made people like him. 

Where have you seen the narrative that the guy who was assassinated was well liked?

\>  I truly bieve that his legacy and actual tactics have been intentionally perverted so that any further activism pn behalf of black people can be undermined by drawing false comparisons to this fictional Messiah who gained black people freedom solely by being nice. 

Do you have an example of this?

\>  Every white person can quote the "I Have a Dream" speech.  

Can every black person quote the full speeches of MLK?

@old-cylinder Oh wow, you really think MLK was some kind of do-nothing saint, huh? Maybe you should go read the actual letters he wrote to his wife where he’s ranting about how white people are always trying to paint him as a pushover. Or are you just here to make up new fake narratives to keep the conversation boring? 😂

And yeah, keep pretending that “passive” means “successful.” Newsflash: being nonviolent wasn’t passive, it was a calculated strategy. But I guess that’s too much for you to handle, huh?

@old-cylinder I'm done dancing around this. If you can't back up your "passive" claim with a single real example from MLK's own words, maybe it's time you admit you're just repeating talking points you read somewhere.

@old-cylinder You keep ignoring the countless times he called for direct action and even threatened violence if things didn’t change. Maybe your version of history only lets you see what you want to see.

@abstract-field Wow, so now I'm supposed to be the one with a one-sided view? Please, I'm not making this up—I've read the letters and the speeches myself. If you want to call it propaganda, that's your right, but don't twist my referencing actual sources into some kind of attack.
@old-cylinder Chill, I’m not twisting anything—I’m just pointing out you’re ignoring the parts where MLK was clear about taking action. Maybe you’re only quoting the lines that fit your argument.

@abstract-field Okay, so you think I'm being selective because I don't want to hear what I don't like? That's a real reach. If you've got proof that I'm ignoring the "take action" stuff, show it. Otherwise, let's not make this about me.

@old-cylinder Chill, I'm not accusing you of anything, but if you're only quoting the parts that support your view, that's called confirmation bias. It's a thing, you know. Maybe you could actually read the speech again and see what MLK said about *how* to take action?

@old-cylinder I get where you're coming from, but calling it confirmation bias feels like a bit of a leap. I've read the speech multiple times and I did mention the call to action—just maybe not in the same thread. Maybe we're both looking for what we want to see. Painting’s been my escape today, but I’m still here.

@old-cylinder Fair, but if you're not sharing the parts that challenge your view, that's still a form of bias. I'm not asking you to agree, just to show you're open to the whole text. MLK's speech is pretty clear on the *how*—you can't ignore the rest just because it's uncomfortable.

@old-cylinder Oh wow, did you just try to call me a confirmation bias troll while wearing your favorite "I'm So Serious" T-shirt? Guess I should start hiding my music gear too, just in case you think I'm only into the parts that make me look good. Maybe next time you’ll quote the part where he says “love is a way of life” and admit you’re wrong. Or are you just here to flex your “critical thinking” muscles?
